name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَـٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٨٠)
```

```english
To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them, and abandon those who commit sacrilege in His names. Soon they shall be requited for what they used to do. (180)
```

```arabic
وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٨١)
```

```english
Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby. (181)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٢)
```

```english
As for those who deny Our signs, We will draw them imperceptibly [into ruin], whence they do not know. (182)
```

```arabic
وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ (١٨٣)
```

```english
And I will grant them respite, for My devising is indeed sure. (183)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ ۗ مَا بِصَاحِبِهِم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٨٤)
```

```english
Have they not reflected that there is no madness in their companion, [and that] he is just a manifest warner? (184)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَنظُرُوا۟ فِى مَلَكُوتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ وَأَنْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَدِ ٱقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٨٥)
```

```english
Have they not contemplated the dominions of the heavens and the earth, and whatever things Allah has created, and that maybe their time has already drawn near? So what discourse will they believe after this?! (185)
```

```arabic
مَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِىَ لَهُۥ ۚ وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١٨٦)
```

```english
Whomever Allah leads astray has no guide, and He leaves them bewildered in their rebellion. (186)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, serving as a renewed clarification of the conclusion reached in the previous verses. This is because guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and misguidance (الضلال) revolve around calling upon Him, the Exalted, by His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) or deviating (الإلحاد) regarding them. People—whether they be adherents of a faith, heretics (زنديق), the learned, or the ignorant—do not differ in their primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and their inner conscience (سريرتهم) in the fact that this witnessed world relies upon a Reality that sustains the essences of its parts and organizes its system. That Reality is Allah, the Glorified, from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything returns; He Who bestows upon the world the beauty and perfection observed within it, all of which belongs to Him and originates from Him.

In this regard, despite their agreement on the fundamental Essence (الذات), people fall into three categories: One category names Him with meanings that encompass only those attributes (الصفات) indicative of perfection or those negating every deficiency and blemish, as is befitting to be ascribed to His presence. A second category deviates (يلحدون) regarding His names, attributing the qualities specific to Him to others, such as the materialists (الماديين) and atheists (الدهريين) who attribute creation, the giving of life, and the provision of sustenance to matter or time. This also includes idolaters who attribute good and benefit to their deities, and some of the People of the Book who describe their prophet or religious leaders with characteristics exclusive to Him, the Exalted. Joined with them is a group of believers who grant cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) a level of independence in influence (التأثير) that is befitting only for Allah, the Glorified. The third category believes in Him, the Exalted, yet they deviate regarding His names by ascribing to Him attributes of deficiency and lowly actions from which He is transcendent (منزه). This includes the belief that He has a body, a location, or that material senses can perceive Him under certain conditions; or that He possesses knowledge like our knowledge, a will like our will, power like our capacities, or that His existence has a temporal duration (بقاء زمانيا) like ours. It also includes ascribing injustice to His actions or ignorance to His judgment and the like; all of these are forms of deviation (الإلحاد) regarding His names.

In reality, these three categories return to two: a category that calls upon Him by the Beautiful Names and worships Allah, the Lord of Majesty and Honor (ذا الجلال والإكرام); these are the ones guided by the truth. The other category deviates regarding His names, naming others by His name or naming Him by the names of others; these are the companions of misguidance whose destination is the Fire, according to their state of misguidance and its degrees. Allah, the Glorified, has clarified that guidance (الهداية) is from Him absolutely, for it is a beautiful attribute and its reality belongs to Him, the Exalted. As for misguidance (الضلال), its origin is not ascribed to Him, the Glorified, because in reality, it is the absence of the locus being guided by the guidance of Allah; it is a privative meaning (معنى عدمي) and an attribute of deficiency. However, its stabilization in the locus after its initial occurrence—making it a necessary attribute of the locus in the sense of withdrawing success (سلب التوفيق) and cutting off divine bestowal as a recompense for the misguided one because they preferred misguidance over guidance and denied the signs of Allah—this is from Allah, the Glorified. He has ascribed this to Himself in His speech, and that is through gradual enticement (الاستدراج) and the granting of respite (الإملاء).

Thus, the verses indicate that the conclusion of His speech—that the reality of guidance and misguidance is from Allah—means that the matter revolves around calling upon Him, the Exalted, by the Beautiful Names, all of which belong to Him, and that is guidance; and deviating regarding His names. People in this regard are of two types: one guided by the guidance of Allah, not turning from Him to another, and one misguided, swerving from His names and denying His signs. Allah, the Glorified, drives them to the Fire as a recompense for their denial of His signs, as He said: **وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ** "Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans" (7:179), and that is through gradual enticement and the granting of respite.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا** "To Allah belong the Best Names, so call Him by them" (7:180). Linguistically, the name (الاسم) is that which points to a thing, whether it conveys a descriptive meaning (معنى وصفياً)—like a word used to refer to a thing because it indicates a meaning existing within it—or it conveys nothing but a reference to the essence (الذات), such as 'Zayd' and 'Amr', especially improvised proper nouns (الأعلام المرتجلة). The description of the names as "Beautiful" (الحسنى)—which is the feminine form of "the best" (أحسن)—indicates that what is meant are the names that contain a descriptive meaning, excluding those that point only to the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية) alone, if such names existed among His names. Nor does it mean every descriptive meaning, but rather the descriptive meaning that contains beauty (الحسن); and not just any beautiful descriptive meaning, but that which is "best" (أحسن) in relation to others when considered with the Transcendent Essence. For instance, "brave" (الشجاع) and "chaste" (العفيف) are beautiful names, but they are not befitting of His holy presence because they imply a physical characteristic (خصوصية جسمانية) that cannot be stripped from them; had it been possible, there would be no barrier to applying them to Him, just as "Generous" (الجواد), "Just" (العدل), and "Merciful" (الرحيم) are applied.

Therefore, for a name among His names to be the "Best Name" (أحسن الأسماء), it must point to a meaning of perfection (معنى كمالي) that is not mixed with deficiency or non-existence (عدم)—a mixing such that the meaning cannot be liberated and purified from that deficiency and non-existence. This applies to everything that necessitates need, non-existence, or lack, such as bodies (الأجسام), physicalities (الجسمانيات), reprehensible or loathsome acts, and privative meanings (المعاني العدمية).

All these names, which are the product of our languages, were only coined for their instances (مصاديقها) among us, which are never free from the taint of need and deficiency. However, some of them are such that the aspects of need and deficiency cannot be stripped from them, such as "body" (الجسم), "color" (اللون), and "dimension" (المقدار). Others are such that this stripping is possible, such as "Knowledge" (العلم), "Life" (الحياة), and "Power" (القدرة). Knowledge in us is the encompassing of a thing by taking its form from the outside through material means; power in us is the origin of an action through a material quality present in our muscles; and life is our being such that we know and act through our means of knowledge and power. These are not befitting of His holy presence. However, if we strip their meanings of material specificities, "Knowledge" becomes the encompassing of a thing by its presence (حضوره) before Him; "Power" becomes the origination of a thing by bringing it into existence (إيجاده); and "Life" becomes a thing being such that it knows and acts. There is no barrier to applying these to Him because they are meanings of perfection free from aspects of deficiency and need. Both reason (العقل) and tradition (النقل) have indicated that every attribute of perfection (صفة كمالية) belongs to Him, the Exalted, and He is the bestower of it upon others without a prior model. Thus, He, the Exalted, is Knowing, Powerful, and Living, but not like our knowledge, power, and life; rather, in a manner befitting His holy presence from the reality of these perfectionist meanings stripped of deficiencies.

The predicate (الخبر) has been placed first in His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** "To Allah belong the Best Names," which signifies exclusivity (الحصر). The word "Names" (الأسماء) is prefixed with the definite article (اللام), and a plural prefixed with the definite article signifies generality (العموم). The implication of this is that every name that is "best" in existence belongs to Allah, the Glorified; no one shares it with Him. Since Allah, the Glorified, ascribes some of these meanings to others and names them by them—such as knowledge, life, creation, and mercy—the meaning of them "belonging to Allah" is that their true reality (حقيقتها) belongs to Him alone, without partner.

The apparent sense of the verses, and indeed the explicit text of some, supports this meaning, such as His saying: **أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** "...that power belongs entirely to Allah" (2:165); and His saying: **فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** "...for all might belongs to Allah" (4:139); and His saying: **وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ** "...and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" (2:255); and His saying: **هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "He is the Living One, there is no god but Him" (40:65). Thus, to Allah, the Glorified, belongs the reality of every "best name"; no one else shares in it except for what He has granted them of it, however He willed and wished.

This meaning is supported by the apparent sense of His speech wherever His names are mentioned in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: **اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** "Allah—there is no god but Him—to Him belong the Best Names" (20:8); and His saying: **قُلِ ادْعُوا اللَّهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمَٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** "Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names’" (17:110); and His saying: **لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "To Him belong the Best Names. Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him" (59:24). The apparent sense of all these verses is that the reality of every "best name" belongs to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

As for the possibility suggested by some that the definite article (اللام) in "the Names" (الأسماء) refers to a specific set (للعهد), there is no evidence for this, nor is there anything in the surrounding context of the verses to support it, other than the narrations known to the proponent that count the Beautiful Names; the discussion regarding these will come in the following traditional discourse (البحث الروائي), Allah willing.

His saying: **فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا** "so call Him by them" is either from "calling" (الدعوة) in the sense of naming (التسمية)—as when we say: "I called him Zayd" or "I called you Abu Abdillah," meaning I named him or named you—or from "calling" in the sense of invocation (النداء), meaning: call upon Him by them, saying: "O Compassionate, O Merciful," and so on. Or it is from "calling" in the sense of worship (العبادة), meaning: worship Him while acknowledging that He is characterized by the beautiful attributes and noble meanings that these names indicate.

Commentators have considered all these meanings, but His speech, the Exalted, in various places where the calling (دعاء) of the Lord is mentioned, supports this last meaning. This is seen in the previously mentioned verse: **قُلِ ادْعُوا اللَّهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمَٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** (17:110); and His saying: **وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ** "Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased" (40:60), where He first mentioned "calling" (الدعاء) and then replaced it with "worship" (العبادة), hinting at their unity. Also, His saying: **وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّن يَدْعُو مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَن لَّا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ وَهُمْ عَن دُعَائِهِمْ غَافِلُونَ وَإِذَا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كَانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْدَاءً وَكَانُوا بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ كَافِرِينَ** "Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such [entities] as will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and who are oblivious of their invocation? When the people are mustered, they will be their enemies, and they will disavow their worship" (46:5-6); and His saying: **هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** "He is the Living One, there is no god but Him. So invoke Him, putting exclusive faith in Him" (40:65), meaning sincerity in worship.

This is further supported by the end of the verse: **وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمَائِهِ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "...and leave those who blaspheme His names. They shall be requited for what they used to do" (7:180). Its apparent sense is that if "calling" meant naming or invocation rather than worship, it would have been more appropriate to say: "for what they used to describe," as He said in another place: **سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ** "...He will soon requite them for their descriptions" (6:139).

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: To Allah belong all the names that are best, so worship Him and turn to Him through them; naming and invocation are among the concomitants of worship.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمَائِهِ** "...and leave those who blaspheme His names" to the end of the verse. Deviation (اللحد) and blasphemy/deviation (الإلحاد) carry the same meaning, which is to swerve or incline away from the center toward one of the sides. From this comes the side-niche of a grave (لحد) because it is on the side, unlike the trench (الضريح) which is in the middle. Thus, the reading *yalhadun* (with a *fatha* on the *ya*) from the simple verb, and *yulhidun* (with a *damma* on the *ya*) from the causative form, carry the same meaning. It is reported from some linguists that *lahd* means inclining to a side, while *ilhad* means disputation and wrangling.

His saying: **سَيُجْزَوْنَ** "They shall be requited..." is a separate sentence because it serves as an answer to an implied question. It is as if when it was said: "and leave those who blaspheme His names," it was asked: "What will become of them?" and the answer was: "They shall be requited for what they used to do." There are remaining points regarding the Beautiful Names that will reach you in a separate discourse we shall present after completing the commentary on the verses, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** "Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby" (7:181). Some of what pertains to this has already been discussed regarding His saying: **وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** "Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby" (7:159). This verse is distinguished by its occurrence in the context of dividing people into the misguided and the guided, and the clarification that the criterion for this is calling upon Him, the Glorified, by the Best Names befitting His presence, versus deviating regarding His names. It indicates that the human species includes a group—be it small or large—that is truly guided. Since the discussion is about true guidance and misguidance rooted in Allah's act—"Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers"—true guidance can only stem from true leading (هداية حقيقية), which belongs to Allah, the Glorified. It has been previously mentioned regarding His saying: **فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ** "...So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will never disbelieve in them" (6:89), and elsewhere, that true divine guidance never fails its requirement in any way and necessitates protection (العصمة) from misguidance. Similarly, the contrast in His saying: **أَفَمَن يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَن يُهْدَىٰ** "...Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who guides not unless he [himself] is guided?..." (10:35) indicates that he who guides to the truth must not be guided by another, but only by Allah; understand this.

Accordingly, ascribing guidance to this nation (أمة) is not without indication of their immunity from misguidance and their protection by Allah from deviation. This is either because all those referred to by His saying: "a nation who guide by the truth" are characterized by this protection (العصمة) and safeguard, such as the prophets and the vicegerents (الأوصياء), or because some of this nation are so, and the whole is described by the attribute of the part, similar to His saying: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood..." (45:16), and His saying: **وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا** "...and made you kings..." (5:20), and His saying: **لِّتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ** "...that you may be witnesses to mankind..." (2:143), whereas only some of them, not all, were characterized by these merits.

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: We do not command you with something non-existent or beyond human capacity, for among those We have created is a nation clothed in true guidance, guiding by the truth because Allah has honored them with His special guidance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** "As for those who deny Our signs, We will draw them on [to destruction] degree by degree whence they do not know" (7:182). Gradual enticement (الاستدراج) is to cause one to ascend or descend degree by degree, or to draw someone near to a matter or place. The context indicates that what is meant here is drawing them near to destruction, either in this world or the Hereafter.

Restricting the gradual enticement to being "whence they do not know" indicates that this approach is hidden and not apparent to them; rather, it is latent within the manifestations of material life in which they amuse themselves. They continue to draw closer to destruction as their wrongdoings intensify; it is the renewal of blessing after blessing until their enjoyment of them distracts them from reflecting on the consequence of their affair, as mentioned in His saying: **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا** "Then We change their hardship into ease, until they multiply..." (7:95). And He, the Exalted, said: **لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلَادِ مَتَاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ** "Do not be deceived by the turnaround of the faithless in the towns. It is a brief enjoyment; then their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place" (3:196-197).

From another perspective, when these people cut themselves off from the remembrance of their Lord and denied His signs, they were stripped of the tranquility (اطمئنان) and security of hearts by clinging to the hem of causes other than Allah. They were punished with the agitation of souls, the anxiety of hearts, the inadequacy of causes, and the accumulation of calamities, while they think this is life, having forgotten the meaning of the reality of a happy life. They continue to increase in the destructive trappings of the world, thereby increasing in punishment while they reckon it an increase in blessing, until they arrive at the punishment of the Hereafter, which is more bitter and more calamitous. Thus, they are drawn on degree by degree in punishment from the moment they denied the signs of their Lord until they meet their day which they are promised.

He, the Exalted, said: **أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ** "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28); and He said: **وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا** "But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life..." (20:124); and He said: **فَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَالُهُمْ وَلَا أَوْلَادُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَافِرُونَ** "So let not their wealth or their children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls should depart while they are faithless" (9:55). This is another meaning of gradual enticement, but His saying afterward: "and I will grant them respite" does not fit this; therefore, the first meaning is the one determined.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ** "And I will grant them respite; indeed My device is firm" (7:183). Granting respite (الإملاء) is to give time. His saying: "indeed My device is firm" is the justification for the sum of what is in the two verses. In His saying: "and I will grant them respite" (وَأُمْلِي) after His saying: "We will draw them on" (سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ), there is a shift (التفات) from the first-person plural to the first-person singular to indicate increased concern with depriving them of divine mercy and leading them to the place of destruction.

Also, granting respite is giving them time until a specified term. Thus, it carries the meaning of His saying: **وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** "...Had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord [postponing their punishment] until a specified time, the matter would have been decided between them..." (42:14). This "decree" (الكلمة) is His saying to Adam (peace be upon him) upon his descent to the earth: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ** "...and there will be for you on the earth an abode and a livelihood for a while" (2:36). This is the divine decree (القضاء), and the decree is exclusive to Him, the Exalted; no one shares it with Him. This is unlike gradual enticement, which is the delivery of blessing after blessing and their renewal, for these are divine blessings bestowed through intermediaries such as angels and the Command (الأمر). For this reason, the first-person plural was used for gradual enticement, while the first-person singular was used for granting respite and for the "device" (الكيد), which is the result obtained from gradual enticement and the granting of respite.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا ۗ مَا بِصَاحِبِهِم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ** "Have they not reflected that there is no madness in their companion? He is just a manifest warner" (7:184). There is significant disagreement among commentators regarding the structure of this speech. What immediately comes to mind from the context is that His saying: "Have they not reflected?" is a complete statement intended for denial and rebuke. Then His saying: "there is no madness in their companion..." is another statement intended to clarify the truthfulness of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his claim to prophethood, pointing to what they should reflect upon. It is as if it were said: Have they not reflected on the fact that there is no madness in their companion, so that this becomes clear to them? Indeed, there is no madness in him; he is but a manifest warner.

The expression referring to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as "their companion" (صاحبهم) points to the material for intellectual reasoning, for he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) accompanied them and they accompanied him throughout his life among them. Had there been any madness in him, it would have certainly been apparent to them. Thus, in what he has brought, he is a warner, not a madman. Madness (الجنة) is a type of insanity, as has been said, though it is possible that it refers to an individual from the jinn, based on their claim that a madman is possessed by some jinn who speaks through him and with his tongue.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَنظُرُوا فِي مَلَكُوتِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "Have they not observed the dominion of the heavens and the earth..." (7:185) to the end of the verse. It has been mentioned repeatedly that the dominion (الملكوت) in the parlance of the Quran—as appears from His saying: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things..." (36:82-83)—is the inner aspect (الوجه الباطن) of things that faces the side of the Lord, the Exalted. Observing this aspect and certainty (اليقين) are inseparable, as understood from His saying: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude" (6:75).

The intent is to rebuke them for turning away and disregarding the dominion-aspect of things. Why did they forget it and not observe it so that it might become clear to them that what he calls them to is the truth?

His saying: **وَمَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ مِن شَيْءٍ** "...and anything that Allah has created?" is conjoined to the position of "the heavens." His saying "of anything" (من شيء) is an explanation of the relative pronoun "what" (ما). The meaning of the verse is: Why did they not observe the creation of the heavens and the earth and anything else that Allah has created? But not from the aspect that faces the things themselves—which would result in knowledge of natural properties—but from the aspect that their existences are not independent in themselves, but linked to another, in need of a Lord Who manages their affair and the affair of everything, and He is the Lord of all the worlds.

His saying: **وَأَنْ عَسَىٰ أَن يَكُونَ قَدِ اقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ** "...and that maybe their term has already drawn near?" is conjoined to His saying: "the dominion" (ملكوت) because it is in the interpretation of a singular noun. The estimation is: Have they not observed that perhaps their term has already drawn near? For observing this possibility might turn them away from persisting in their misguidance and error. Most of what turns a human away from occupying themselves with the affair of the Hereafter and directs them toward being deceived by the world is the forgetting of death, whose messenger one knows not when he shall arrive. But if one pays attention to that and witnesses their ignorance of their term, and that it is hoped and possible that it has drawn near to them, it cuts off the roots of heedlessness and prevents them from following passion and long hopes.

His saying: **فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ** "So in what discourse will they believe after this?" (7:185). The pronoun refers to the Quran, as required by the context. The speech conveys despair regarding their ever believing. That is, if they do not believe in the Quran—which is His manifestation (تجليه) to them through His speech, speaking to them with arguments, proofs, and goodly exhortation that compel their intellects to accept, while it is also a dazzling miracle—then they will never believe in anything else. Allah, the Glorified, has already informed that He has set a seal upon their hearts, so there is no way for them to understand the word or believe in the truth. Therefore, He followed it with His saying in the next verse: **مَن يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِيَ لَهُ** "Whomever Allah leads astray, there is no guide for him..." (7:186).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَن يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِيَ لَهُ ۚ وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيَانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ** "Whomever Allah leads astray, there is no guide for him; and He leaves them in their rebellion, bewildered" (7:186). Bewilderment (العمه) is confusion and hesitation in misguidance or the lack of knowledge of the proof. The opposite—that whomever He guides, there is no misguider for him—was not mentioned because the speech is intended to justify the previous verse: "So in what discourse..." It is as if it were said: Why do they not believe in any discourse at all? And it was replied: Because whomever Allah leads astray... to the end of the verse.

## A Discourse on the Beautiful Names in Several Sections

**1 - What is the meaning of the Beautiful Names?** And what is the path to them? When we first open our eyes and witness the scenes of existence, our perception falls upon ourselves and the closest matters to us, which are our connections with the external universe necessitated by our powers working for our survival. Thus, our selves, our powers, and our actions related to them are the first to knock on the door of our perception. However, we do not see our selves except as connected to others, nor our powers or actions except as such. Thus, need (الحاجة) is among the oldest things a human witnesses; they witness it from themselves and from everything connected to them of their powers, actions, and the external world. At that point, one judges that there is an Essence (ذات) that fulfills their need and closes their gap, and to Whom everything ends, and He is Allah, the Glorified. Our observation and judgment are confirmed by His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ ۖ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ** "O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient..." (35:15).

History has failed to find the beginning of the emergence of the belief in Lordship (الربوبية) among human individuals; rather, it found it accompanying humanity to the oldest eras that have passed over this species. Even the primitive peoples who mimic early humans in simplicity—when they were discovered in the corners of the globe, such as the inhabitants of America and Australia—were found to have a belief in high powers beyond the level of nature that they adhered to. This is a belief in Lordship, even if they were mistaken about the specific instance (المصداق). Thus, the acknowledgment of an Essence to which the affair of everything ends is among the requirements of the human primordial nature (الفطرة); no one swerves from it except one who has deviated from the inspiration of their nature due to a doubt that occurred to them, like one who forces themselves to become accustomed to poison while their nature warns them through its inspiration, yet they find beautiful that which they have been afflicted with.

Furthermore, the oldest thing we face in the search for divine knowledge is that we acknowledge the ending of everything to Him, and that its being and existence are from Him. Thus, He owns everything, for we know that if He did not own them, He could not bestow them or grant them to others. Moreover, some of these things are such that their reality is built only upon need and indicative of deficiency, while He, the Exalted, is transcendent (منزه) from every need and deficiency, because He is the One to Whom everything returns for the removal of its need and deficiency.

To Him belongs ownership (الملك) — *sovereignty and possession* — in an absolute sense. Thus, He, the Glorified, owns every attribute of perfection (صفة كمال) we find in existence, such as life, power, knowledge, hearing, sight, provision, mercy, might, and so on.

He, the Glorified, is Living, Powerful, Knowing, Hearing, and Seeing, because in their negation is the affirmation of deficiency, and there is no way for deficiency to reach Him. He is the Provider, Merciful, Mighty, Giver of Life, Giver of Death, Originator, Restorer, and Resurrector, and so on, because provision, mercy, might, giving life, giving death, originating, restoring, and resurrecting belong to Him. He is the All-Glorified (السبوح), the All-Holy (القدوس), the Most High (العلي), the Great (الكبير), the Transcendent (المتعال), and so on; by these, we mean the negation of every privative attribute (نعت عدمي) and every attribute of deficiency from Him.

This is our path to affirming the names and attributes for Him, the Exalted, in its simplicity. The Book of Allah has confirmed this for us, as it has affirmed ownership (الملك) and sovereignty (الملك) for Him absolutely in many verses that need not be cited.

**2 - What is the limit of what we describe Him with** or name Him by? It became clear from the first section that we negate from Him the aspects of deficiency and need that we find in what we witness of the parts of the world, which are the opposites of perfection, such as death, lack, poverty, humiliation, incapacity, ignorance, and the like. It is known that the negation of these things—which are in themselves negative—returns to the affirmation of perfection. For in the negation of poverty is the affirmation of wealth, and in the negation of humiliation, incapacity, and ignorance is the affirmation of might, power, and knowledge, and so on.

As for the attributes of perfection that we affirm for Him, the Glorified—such as life, power, knowledge, and the like—you have learned that we affirm them by acknowledging His ownership of all perfections established in the realm of existence. However, we negate from Him, the Exalted, the aspects of need and deficiency that accompany these attributes according to their existence in their instances (مصاديقها).

Knowledge in a human, for example, is an encompassing of the known through the presence (إحاطة حضورية) of an abstracted form taken by bodily powers from the outside. What is befitting of His presence is the fundamental meaning of encompassing-by-presence. As for it being through the taking of a form—which necessitates the prior existence of the known in the outside and material bodily instruments—this is part of the deficiency from which He, the Exalted, must be sanctified. In short, we affirm for Him the fundamental positive meaning (أصل المعنى الثبوتي) and strip away the specificity of the instance (خصوصية المصداق) that leads to deficiency and need.

Furthermore, since we have negated from Him every deficiency and need—and it is a deficiency for a thing to be limited by a boundary (محدودا بحد), its existence ending at a terminus—for a thing does not limit itself; rather, it is limited by another that overpowers it by imposing the boundary and terminus. Therefore, we have negated from Him every boundary and terminus. He, the Glorified, is not limited in His essence by anything, nor in His attributes by anything. He, the Exalted, said: **وَهُوَ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** "...and He is the One, the All-paramount" (13:16). To Him belongs the unity (الوحدة) that overpowers everything from His side and thus encompasses it.

From here, we judge that His attributes, the Exalted, are identical to His essence (عين ذاته), and every attribute is identical to every other attribute; there is no distinction except in terms of concept (المفهوم). If His knowledge were other than His power, for example, and each of them were other than His essence—as is the case in us humans—each of them would limit the other, and the other would end at it. Thus, there would be the limited and the limit, the finite and the finish, resulting in composition (تركيب) and need for a limiter to limit it; far exalted is He above that and sanctified. This is the attribute of His oneness (أحديته); He is not divided from any aspect, nor does He become multiple in external reality or in the mind.

From what has preceded, the falsity of the view of those who said that the meanings of His attributes, the Exalted, return to negation (النفي) becomes apparent—out of caution to sanctify Him from the attributes of His creation. Thus, the meaning of knowledge, power, and life there is the absence of ignorance, incapacity, and death, and likewise for other high attributes. This is because it necessitates the negation of all attributes of perfection from Him, the Exalted. You have learned that our primordial path (سلوكنا الفطري) rejects that, and the apparent senses of the noble verses contradict it. Similar to this is the view that His attributes are additional to His essence, or the negation of attributes and the affirmation of their effects, and other things that have been said regarding the attributes. All of these are refuted by what has preceded regarding the nature of our primordial path; the detailed discussion of their invalidity belongs elsewhere.

**3 - Their Classifications:** It appears from what we have presented regarding the nature of the primordial path that among the attributes of Allah, the Glorified, are those that convey a positive meaning (معنى ثبوتياً), such as knowledge and life, which are those encompassing the meaning of perfection. Others convey a meaning of negation (معنى السلب), which are for sanctification (التنزيه), such as the All-Glorified (السبوح) and the All-Holy (القدوس). Thus, the classification of attributes into two types is completed: positive (ثبوتية) and negative (سلبية).

Also, among the attributes are those that are identical to the essence, not additional to it, such as life, power, and knowledge of the essence; these are the essential attributes (الصفات الذاتية). Others require for their realization the assumption of the realization of the essence beforehand, such as creation (الخلق) and provision (الرزق); these are the active attributes (الصفات الفعلية). They are additional to the essence, abstracted from the level of action (مقام الفعل). The meaning of their abstraction from that level is that we, for example, find these blessings that we enjoy and move within; their relation to Allah, the Glorified, is like the relation of the provision (الرزق) assigned to the army by the king to the king himself, so we call it provision. Since it ends with Him, the Exalted, we call Him the Provider (رازقاً). Similar to this are creation, mercy, forgiveness, and all other active attributes and names. They are applied to Him, the Exalted, and He is named by them without Him being characterized by their meanings in the same way He is characterized by life, power, and other essential attributes. Had He been characterized by them in reality, they would have been essential attributes not outside the essence. Thus, for the attributes and names, there is another classification into essential (ذاتية) and active (فعلية).

They have another classification into substantive (نفسية) and relative (إضافية). That which has no relation in its meaning to what is outside the level of the essence, such as life, is substantive. That which has a relation to the outside—whether it is a substantive meaning with a relation, such as making (الصنع) and creation (الخلق), which are substantive-with-relation, or a purely relative meaning, such as being the Creator (الخالقية) and being the Provider (الرازقية)—is purely relative.

**4 - The Relations of the Attributes and Names** to us and their relations among themselves. There is no difference between the attribute (الصفة) and the name (الاسم) except that the attribute points to one of the meanings with which the essence is characterized—broader than identity or otherness—while the name is that which points to the essence taken with an attribute. Thus, life and knowledge are two attributes, while the Living (الحي) and the Knowing (العالم) are two names. Since the word has no function except to point to the meaning and reveal it, the reality of the attribute and the name is that which the word for the attribute and the name reveals. Thus, the reality of life pointed to by the word "life" is the divine attribute, which is identical to the essence. The reality of the essence with its life—which is its very self—is the divine name. From this perspective, the Living and Life return to being two names for the name and the attribute, even if by the previous perspective they were the name itself and the attribute itself.

It has been mentioned that in our primordial path toward the names, we only became aware of them through what we witnessed in the universe of attributes of perfection. We became certain from that that Allah, the Glorified, is named by them because He is their owner Who bestowed them upon us. And from what we witnessed in it of attributes of deficiency and need, we became certain that He, the Exalted, is transcendent from them, characterized by their opposites of attributes of perfection, and through them, He removes from us deficiency and need where He removes them. Thus, witnessing knowledge and power in the universe guides us to the certainty that to Him, the Glorified, belong knowledge and power through which He bestows what He bestows of knowledge and power. Witnessing ignorance and incapacity in existence points us to the fact that He is transcendent from them, characterized by their opposites of knowledge and power through which our need for knowledge and power is removed where it is removed, and so on for the rest.

From here, it appears that the aspects of creation and the specificities of existence present in things are linked to His Transcendent Essence through His noble attributes. That is, the attributes are intermediaries (وسائط) between the essence and His handiworks. Thus, the knowledge, power, provision, and blessing that we have are bestowed by Him, the Glorified, in His capacity as Knowing, Powerful, Provider, and Bestower, in order. Our ignorance is removed by His knowledge, our incapacity by His power, our humiliation by His might, our poverty by His wealth, and our sins by His pardon and forgiveness. If you wish, say from another perspective: He overpowers us by His overpowering (قهره), limits us by His illimitability, terminates us by His infinity, humbles us by His loftiness, and abases us by His might. He judges within us as He wills by His sovereignty (ملكه) — *His kingship* — and disposes of us as He wills by His ownership (ملكه) — *His possession*; understand this.

This is what we follow according to the taste derived from the pure primordial nature. One who asks Allah for wealth does not say: "O Giver of Death, O Humiliator, enrich me"; rather, they call upon Him by His names: the Wealthy (الغني), the Mighty (العزيز), and the Powerful (القادر), for example. The sick person who turns to Him for the healing of their illness says: "O Healer, O Granter of Health, O Kind, O Merciful, have mercy on me and heal me"; they will not say: "O Giver of Death, O Avenger, O Possessor of Might, heal me," and so on.

The noble Quran confirms us in this path and judgment; it is the truest witness to the correctness of this view. You see it concluding its noble verses with what suits the contents of their texts of the divine names, and justifying the truths it presents by mentioning a name or two of the names according to what the case requires. The Quran is the only heavenly book that employs the divine names in establishing its objectives, and it teaches us the science of the names (علم الأسماء) among the heavenly books attributed to revelation that have reached us.

Thus, it has become clear that we are related to Him, the Exalted, through the medium of His names, and to His names through the medium of their effects spread throughout the regions of our witnessed world. The effects of beauty (الجمال) and majesty (الجلال) in this world are what link us to the names of His beauty and majesty—of life, knowledge, power, might, greatness, and pride. Then the names relate us to the Transcendent Essence upon which all parts of the world rely for their independence.

These effects that we have from the side of His names, the Exalted, are different in themselves in terms of breadth and narrowness, corresponding to the generality and specificity in their concepts. The gift of knowledge that we have branches out into hearing, sight, imagination, and intellection, for example. Then these, along with power, life, and others, fall under provision (الرزق), giving (الإعطاء), bestowing (الإنعام), and generosity (الجود). Then these, along with pardon and forgiveness and the like, fall under the general mercy (الرحمة العامة).

From here, it appears that among the names themselves, there is breadth and narrowness, generality and specificity, in the same order as between their effects existing in our world. Some are specific (خاصة) and some are general (عامة). Their specificity and generality are by the specificity of their realities—which their effects reveal—and their generality. The relations between their concepts reveal the nature of the relations between their realities. Thus, "Knowledge" (العلم) is a specific name in relation to "the Living" (الحي) and general in relation to "the Hearing" (السميع), "the Seeing" (البصير), "the Witness" (الشهيد), "the Subtle" (اللطيف), and "the Aware" (الخبير). "The Provider" (الرازق) is specific in relation to "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) and general in relation to "the Healer" (الشافي), "the Helper" (الناصر), and "the Guide" (الهادي), and so on.

Thus, for the Beautiful Names, there is a vast range that ends at the bottom with a name or specific names under which no other name enters. Then they increase in breadth and generality, so above every name is one that is broader and more general, until they end at the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأكبر) which alone encompasses all the realities of the names, and under which all the scattered realities enter entirely. This is what we usually call the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم).

It is known that the more general a name is, the broader its effects in the world and the greater and more complete the blessings descending from it, because the effects belong to the names as you have learned. Whatever state of generality or specificity is in the name, its effect corresponds to it exactly. Thus, the Greatest Name is that to which every effect ends and to which every matter submits.

**5 - What is the meaning of the Greatest Name?** It has become common among people that it is a verbal name (اسم لفظي) among the names of Allah, the Glorified, which, if called upon, is answered, and nothing escapes its effect. However, since they did not find this property in any of the known Beautiful Names nor in the word "Allah" (لفظ الجلالة), they believed it was composed of unknown letters in an unknown arrangement to us; if we were to find it, we would make everything submit to our will.

In the imagination of the practitioners of incantations and invocations (أصحاب العزائم والدعوات), it has a word that points to it by its nature, not by linguistic convention. However, its letters and their arrangement differ according to the needs and demands. They have specific methods for obtaining it by which they first extract letters and then arrange them and call with them, as known to those who consult their art.

In some of the transmitted narrations, there is a hint of this, such as what was reported: that "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful" is closer to the Greatest Name of Allah than the white of the eye to its black. And what was reported: that it is in the Verse of the Throne (آية الكرسي) and the beginning of Surah Al-Imran. And what was reported: that its letters are scattered in Surah Al-Hamd—the Imam knows them, and if he wishes, he arranges them and calls with them, and he is answered.

And what was reported: that Asif ibn Barkhiya, the minister of Solomon, called—with what he had of the letters of the Greatest Name of Allah—and brought the throne of the Queen of Sheba to Solomon in less than the blink of an eye. And what was reported: that the Greatest Name is composed of seventy-three letters—Allah distributed seventy-two of them among His prophets and reserved one for Himself in the knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب); among other narrations hinting that it has a verbal composition.

The true investigation into cause and effect (العلة والمعلول) and their properties refutes all of this. For true influence (التأثير الحقيقي) revolves around the existence of things in its strength and weakness, and the affinity (المسانخة) between the influencer and the influenced. The verbal name—if we consider it from the aspect of its specific word—is a collection of heard sounds which are among the accidental qualities (الكيفيات العرضية). If it is considered from the aspect of its conceptualized meaning, it is a mental image (صورة ذهنية) that has no effect from its own self on anything at all. It is impossible for a sound we produced through the larynx or an imaginary image we depict in our mind to be such that it overpowers by its existence the existence of everything, and disposes of what we want as we want—turning the sky into earth and the earth into sky, and transforming the world into the Hereafter and vice versa, and so on—while it is itself an effect (معلول) of our will.

The divine names and His Greatest Name specifically—even if they are influential in the universe and are intermediaries and causes for the descent of grace from the Transcendent Essence into this witnessed world—only influence through their realities (بحقائقها), not by the words pointing to them in such-and-such a language, nor by their meanings understood from their words conceptualized in the minds. The meaning of that is that Allah, the Glorified, is the Agent (الفاعل) Who brings everything into existence through His noble attribute suitable for it, which the suitable name contains; not the influence of a word or a concept in the mind or another reality other than the Transcendent Essence.

However, Allah, the Glorified, has promised to answer the call of one who calls Him, as in His saying: **أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ** "I answer the bid of the supplicant when he calls Me" (2:186). This depends on a true calling and seeking, and that the calling and seeking be from Him, the Exalted, not from another—as preceded in the commentary on the verse. So whoever cuts themselves off from every cause and connects to their Lord for one of their needs has connected to the reality of the name suitable for their need. Thus, the name influences through its reality and they are answered. That is the reality of calling by the name. According to the state of the name to which the caller has cut themselves off, the state of influence will be in terms of specificity and generality. If this name were the Greatest Name, everything would submit to its reality and the caller's prayer would be answered absolutely. Upon this must be interpreted what has been reported of narrations and supplications in this chapter, not the verbal name or its concept.

The meaning of Him, the Exalted, teaching a prophet among His prophets or a servant among His servants a name among His names or something of the Greatest Name is that He opens for them the path of cutting oneself off (الانقطاع) to Him, the Exalted, by that name in their calling and asking. If there is a verbal name and it has an understood meaning, that is only because words and their meanings are means and causes by which realities are preserved in a way; understand this.

Know that the specific name might be applied to what is not named by other than Allah, the Glorified, as has been said regarding the two names: Allah, and the All-Beneficent (الرحمن). As for the word "Allah" (لفظ الجلالة), it is a proper name (علم) for Him, the Exalted, specific to Him; it is not a name in the sense we are discussing. As for "the All-Beneficent," you have learned that its meaning is shared between Him and others, the Exalted, since it is among the Beautiful Names. This is from the perspective of exegetical investigation; as for the perspective of legal/jurisprudential (فقهي) view, it is outside our discussion.

**6 - The Number of the Beautiful Names:** There is no evidence in the noble verses for a determined number of the Beautiful Names by which they are limited. Rather, the apparent sense of His saying: **اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** (20:8), and His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا** (7:180), and His saying: **لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (59:24), and similar verses, is that every name in existence is the "best of names" in its meaning, so it belongs to Him, the Exalted. Thus, His Beautiful Names are not limited by a boundary.

It has been mentioned that the apparent sense of His saying: "To Allah belong the Beautiful Names" and "To Him belong the Beautiful Names" is that the meanings of these names belong to Him, the Exalted, in reality and by way of originality (الأصالة), and to others than Him, the Exalted, by way of following (التبع). Thus, He is their owner in reality, and others have nothing of them except what Allah has granted them. Even then, He is the owner of what He has granted others; it did not leave His ownership through the granting. Thus, to Him, the Glorified, belongs the reality of knowledge, for example, and others have nothing of it except what He has gifted them, and even then, it belongs to Him and has not left His ownership and authority.

Among the evidences for the shared meaning (الاشتراك المعنوي) in what is applied to Him, the Exalted, and to others of names and attributes is what has appeared of His names, the Exalted, in the superlative form (أفعل التفضيل), such as "the Most High" (الأعلى) and "the Most Generous" (الأكرم). For the superlative form indicates by its apparent sense the sharing of the preferred and the one preferred over in the fundamental meaning. Likewise, what has appeared by way of genitive construction (الإضافة), such as "the Best of judges," "the Best of providers," and "the Best of creators," due to its appearance in sharing.

**7 - Are the Names of Allah Restricted to Revelation (توقيفية)?** It has become clear from what preceded that there is no evidence for the names of Allah being restricted to revelation from His speech; rather, the matter is the opposite. That which was used as evidence for restriction from His saying: "To Allah belong the Best Names, so call Him by them, and leave those who blaspheme His names..." is based on the definite article (اللام) being for a specific set (للعهد), and that the meaning of blasphemy/deviation (الإلحاد) is to overstep toward what has not appeared of His names through tradition (السمع). Both matters are subject to scrutiny, as has been explained.

As for what has been reported extensively (مستفيضًا) in narrations by both groups [Sunni and Shi‘a] from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Indeed, Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred minus one—whoever enumerates them (أحصاها) shall enter Paradise," or similar wordings, there is no indication in them for the principle of restriction to transmitted names (التوقيف). This is from the perspective of exegetical inquiry (البحث التفسيري). As for the legal inquiry (البحث الفقهي), its reference is the discipline of Jurisprudence (الفقه), and precaution in religion (الاحتياط في الدين) necessitates restricting oneself in naming (التسمية) to what has been received through transmission (السماع). However, as for mere application and attribution (الإجراء والإطلاق) without formal naming, the matter is flexible.

## (Narrative Discourse)

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Rida, from his forefathers, from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام): "Indeed, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, there are ninety-nine names—whoever supplicates to Allah by them, He answers him; and whoever enumerates them (أحصاها) enters Paradise."

**I say:** A similar report from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) will follow through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام). The meaning of his saying: "whoever enumerates them (أحصاها) enters Paradise" is to have faith (الإيمان) in His being characterized by all that those names signify, such that nothing of them is excluded.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is recorded by al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Khuzaymah, Abu ‘Awanah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Tabarani, Abu ‘Abdullah ibn Mandah in *al-Tawhid*, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi in the *Book of Names and Attributes*, from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred minus one—whoever enumerates them enters Paradise. Indeed, He is an odd number (وتر) and He loves the odd.'"

**I say:** It is narrated from Abu Nu‘aym and Ibn Mardawayh from him with the wording: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Allah has one hundred names minus one—whoever supplicates by them, Allah answers his supplication.'" In the same source, Abu Nu‘aym and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas and Ibn ‘Umar, who both said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred minus one—whoever enumerates them enters Paradise.'"
