All-Sufficient (الغني), the Sovereign (المليك), the All-Determiner (المقتدر), the Most Generous (الأكرم), the All-Kind (الرءوف), the Disposer of Affairs (المدبر), the Owner (المالك), the Subduer (القاهر), the Guide (الهادي), the All-Appreciative (الشاكر), the All-Generous (الكريم), the Exalted (الرفيع), the Witness (الشهيد), the One (الواحد), the Possessor of Abundance (ذا الطول), the Lord of the Ways of Ascent (ذا المعارج), the Possessor of Grace (ذا الفضل), the All-Creator (الخلاق), the Surety (الكفيل), the Majestic (الجليل).'"

**I say:** The mention of the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) [Allah] in these narrations containing the enumeration is for the purpose of applying the names to it; otherwise, it is outside the count.

In the same source, Abu Nu‘aym recorded from Muhammad ibn Ja‘far, who said: "I asked my father, Ja‘far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq, about the ninety-nine names—those which, if enumerated, one enters Paradise—and he said: 'They are in the Quran: In *al-Fatihah* there are five names: O Allah (يا الله), O Lord (يا رب), O All-Beneficent (يا رحمن), O Most Merciful (يا رحيم), O Master (يا مالك). In *al-Baqarah* there are thirty-three names: O All-Encompassing (يا محيط), O All-Powerful (يا قدير), O All-Knowing (يا عليم), O All-Wise (يا حكيم), O High (يا علي), O All-Magnificent (يا عظيم), O Ever-Relenting (يا تواب), O All-Seeing (يا بصير), O Guardian (يا ولي), O All-Embracing (يا واسع), O All-Sufficient (يا كافي), O All-Kind (يا رءوف), O Originator (يا بديع), O All-Appreciative (يا شاكر), O One (يا واحد), O All-Hearing (يا سميع), O Withholder (يا قابض), O Expander (يا باسط), O Ever-Living (يا حي), O Self-Subsisting (يا قيوم), O All-Sufficient (يا غني), O All-Praiseworthy (يا حميد), O All-Forgiving (يا غفور), O All-Clement (يا حليم), O Deity (يا إله), O All-Near (يا قريب), O Responder (يا مجيب), O All-Mighty (يا عزيز), O Helper (يا نصير), O All-Strong (يا قوي), O Severe (يا شديد), O Swift (يا سريع), O All-Aware (يا خبير).

In *Al ‘Imran*: O All-Bestower (يا وهاب), O Sustainer (يا قائم), O Truthful (يا صادق), O Resurrector (يا باعث), O Bestower of Blessings (يا منعم), O Bestower of Grace (يا متفضل). In *al-Nisa’*: O All-Watcher (يا رقيب), O Reckoner (يا حسيب), O Witness (يا شهيد), O Nourisher (يا مقيت), O Trustee (يا وكيل), O High (يا علي), O All-Great (يا كبير). In *al-An‘am*: O Creator (يا فاطر), O Subduer (يا قاهر), O All-Subtle (يا لطيف), O Proof (يا برهان). In *al-A‘raf*: O Giver of Life (يا محيي), O Giver of Death (يا مميت). In *al-Anfal*: O Excellent Patron (يا نعم المولى), O Excellent Helper (يا نعم النصير). In *Hud*: O All-Preserver (يا حفيظ), O All-Glorious (يا مجيد), O All-Loving (يا ودود), O Doer of what He wills (يا فعالا لما يريد). In *al-Ra‘d*: O All-Great (يا كبير), O Most Exalted (يا متعال). In *Ibrahim*: O All-Bounteous (يا منان), O Inheritor (يا وارث). In *al-Hijr*: O All-Creator (يا خلاق).

In *Maryam*: O Singular (يا فرد). In *Ta Ha*: O All-Forgiver (يا غفار). In *al-Mu’minun*: O All-Generous (يا كريم). In *al-Nur*: O Truth (يا حق), O Manifest (يا مبين). In *al-Furqan*: O Guide (يا هادي). In *Saba’*: O Opener (يا فتاح). In *al-Zumar*: O Knower (يا عالم). In *Ghafir*: O Forgiver (يا غافر), O Accepter of Repentance (يا قابل التوب), O Possessor of Abundance (يا ذا الطول), O Exalted (يا رفيع). In *al-Dhariyat*: O Provider (يا رزاق), O Possessor of Power (يا ذا القوة), O Firm (يا متين). In *al-Tur*: O All-Benign (يا بر).

In *al-Qamar*: O Sovereign (يا مليك), O All-Determiner (يا مقتدر). In *al-Rahman*: O Lord of Majesty and Generosity (يا ذا الجلال والإكرام), O Lord of the two Easts (يا رب المشرقين), O Lord of the two Wests (يا رب المغربين), O Everlasting (يا باقي), O All-Beneficent (يا محسن). In *al-Hadid*: O First (يا أول), O Last (يا آخر), O Manifest (يا ظاهر), O Hidden (يا باطن). In *al-Hashr*: O King (يا ملك), O All-Holy (يا قدوس), O Peace (يا سلام), O Giver of Faith (يا مؤمن), O Overseer (يا مهيمن), O All-Mighty (يا عزيز), O Compeller (يا جبار), O All-Magnificent (يا متكبر), O Creator (يا خالق), O Maker (يا بارئ), O Shaper (يا مصور). In *al-Buruj*: O Originator (يا مبدئ), O Restorer (يا معيد). In *al-Fajr*: O Odd (يا وتر). In *al-Ikhlas*: O Unique (يا أحد), O Besought (يا صمد).'"

**I say:** The narration is not free from confusion, for it includes the Majestic Name [Allah] among the ninety-nine names when it is not of them [in the count], and it repeats some names like "the All-Great" (الكبير). It mentions ninety-nine at the beginning but concludes with one hundred and ten names. Furthermore, there are other points of contention regarding the presence of certain names in specific Surahs, such as "the Singular" (الفرد) in Surah Maryam and "the Proof" (البرهان) in Surah al-An‘am, among others.

In any case, it has become clear to you from these narrations—which are the ones we have come across regarding the narrations of enumeration—that they do not indicate the restriction of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) to what they enumerate, especially given the variation in the names, the mention of some that are not in the Quran in the form of a name, and the omission of some that are in the Quran in the form of a name. Rather, the most they indicate is that among the names of Allah are ninety-nine whose special property is that whoever supplicates by them is answered, and whoever enumerates them enters Paradise.

Moreover, there are other narrations indicating that His names are more than ninety-nine, as some will follow. In the supplications narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), there are many names of Allah other than what appeared in the Quran or was counted in the narrations of enumeration.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not voiced, with a wording that is not spoken, with a person (بالشخص) that is not embodied, with a likeness that is not described, and with a color that is not dyed—negated from it are directions (الأقطار), distanced from it are limits (الحدود), veiled from it is the sense of every imaginer, hidden but not concealed.

So He made it a complete word (كلمة تامة) consisting of four parts together—none of them being before the other. Then He manifested three names from it due to the need (فاقة) of the creation for them, and He veiled one of them, which is the Hidden and Treasured Name (الاسم المكنون والمخزون). These are the names that were manifested: the Manifest is Allah (الله), the Blessed (تبارك), and the Exalted (تعالى). And He, the Glorified, subjected four pillars (أركان) for each of these names—making twelve pillars. Then He created thirty names for each pillar—acts attributed to them: He is the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), the Most Merciful (الرحيم), the King (الملك), the All-Holy (القدوس), the Creator (الخالق), the Maker (البارئ), the Shaper (المصور), the Ever-Living (الحي), the Self-Subsisting (القيوم), 'neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him,' the All-Knowing (العليم), the All-Aware (الخبير), the All-Hearing (السميع), the All-Wise (الحكيم), the All-Mighty (العزيز), the Compeller (الجبار), the All-Magnificent (المتكبر), the High (العلي), the All-Magnificent (العظيم), the All-Determiner (المقتدر), the All-Powerful (القدير), the Peace (السلام), the Giver of Faith (المؤمن), the Overseer (المهيمن), the Maker (البارئ), the Originator (المنشئ), the Originator (البديع), the Exalted (الرفيع), the Majestic (الجليل), the All-Generous (الكريم), the Provider (الرازق), the Giver of Life (المحيي), the Giver of Death (المميت), the Resurrector (الباعث), the Inheritor (الوارث).

These names and whatever other Beautiful Names there are—until they complete three hundred and sixty names—are an attribution (نسبة) to these three names, and these three names are pillars. And He veiled the one Hidden and Treasured Name by these three names, and that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى** (Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the best names.’ [17:110])"

**I say:** His saying (عليه‌السلام): "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not voiced..."—these enumerated attributes are explicit in that what is meant by this "name" is not the spoken word, nor a meaning signified by the word insofar as it is a mental concept (مفهوم ذهني). For the word and the mental concept it signifies cannot be described by the attributes he used to describe it, as is evident. Likewise, what he mentioned later in the narration precludes this. Thus, the "name" here refers only to the referent (المصداق) that corresponds to the word if there were a word. It is well-known that the name in this sense—especially considering its division into "Allah," "the Blessed," and "the Exalted"—is nothing but the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية) or is subsistent through It, not being entirely external to It.

The attribution of "creation" (الخلق) to this name in his saying "He created a name" reveals that what is meant by creation is other than its conventional meaning, and that what is meant is the manifestation (ظهور) of the Transcendent Essence in a manifestation through which a name among the names arises. At that point, the report aligns with what was previously explained: that the names are ordered among themselves, some being intermediaries for the establishment of others, and they ultimately terminate at a name whose determination (تعين) is the very absence of determination, and the restriction of the Transcendent Essence by it is the very absence of Its being restricted by any limit.

**His saying:** "the Manifest is Allah, the Blessed, and the Exalted" is an allusion to the general aspects to which all specific aspects of perfection terminate, and which the creation needs from all sides of its poverty and need. These are three: the aspect of the Essence's encompassing of every perfection, which is signified by the Majestic Name [Allah]; the aspect of the establishment of perfections and the source of goodness and blessings, which is signified by the name "the Blessed" (تبارك); and the aspect of the negation of deficiencies and the transcendence above needs, which is signified by the word "the Exalted" (تعالى).

**His saying:** "acts attributed to them" (فعلا منسوبا إليها)—meaning to the names—is an allusion to what we previously mentioned regarding the emergence of one name from another. His saying: "until they complete three hundred and sixty names" is explicit in the non-restriction of the Divine names to ninety-nine.

**His saying:** "And these three names are pillars and veils..." etc. Since the Hidden and Treasured Name is a name, it is a determination and manifestation of the Transcendent Essence. Since it is hidden according to its essence and not manifest according to itself, its manifestation is the very absence of its manifestation, and its determination is the very absence of its determination. This is what we sometimes express by saying: He, the Exalted, is not limited by a limit, even by this negative limit; no description or attribute encompasses Him, even this privative description. This itself is a characterization from us, while the Transcendent Essence is greater and more magnificent than it.

The necessary consequence of this is that the Majestic Name [Allah], which reveals the Essence that encompasses all attributes of perfection, is a name among the names of the Essence, below It and below this Hidden and Treasured Name. Likewise, "the Blessed" and "the Exalted" are three names together serving as guardians and veils for the Hidden Name, without any of them preceding the other. These three veils and the Hidden Name veiled by them are all below the Essence. As for the Essence Itself, no allusion (إشارة) reaches It and no expression (عبارة) applies to It, for whatever an expression narrates or an allusion points to is a name among the names, limited in this manner, while the Transcendent Essence is higher and more sublime than it.

**His saying:** "and that is His saying, the Exalted: **قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى**"—the way this is derived is that the pronoun in His saying "to Him" (فَلَهُ) refers back to "Whichever" (أَيًّا), which is a conditional noun among the metonyms (الكنايات) that has no determination for its meaning except the absence of determination. It is well-known that what is meant by "Allah" and "the All-Beneficent" in the verse is the referent (مصداق) of the two words, not the words themselves, for He did not say "call by Allah" or "by the All-Beneficent," but rather "Invoke Allah." Thus, the meaning of the verse is that the names are all attributed to and subsistent in a station about which there is no report and to which there is no allusion except by the absence of report and allusion; so understand that.

In the narration, "the Blessed" (تبارك), "the Exalted" (تعالى), and "neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him" are taken as names, which is based on mere indication of the Essence taken with one of Its attributes, without observing technical linguistic conventions.

The narration is among the most precious of reports (غرر الروايات), pointing to a matter that is deeper in scope than the level of general discussions and conventional understandings. For this reason, we have restricted ourselves in explaining the narration to mere allusions. As for a complete clarification, it cannot be achieved except through an extensive discussion beyond the scope of this place. However, it is based on nothing more than what preceded in the discussion regarding the relations of the names and attributes to us and the relations between them, which was placed in the fourth section of the discourse on the names. So, hold onto it until the matter becomes truly clear to you, and Allah is the Granter of success.

In *Basa’ir al-Darajat*, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) consists of seventy-three letters. Asaf [ibn Barkhiya] possessed only one letter of it, so he spoke it—and the earth between him and the throne of Bilqis sank—then he took the throne with his hand, and the earth returned to how it was, faster than the blink of an eye. We have seventy-two letters of the Name, and one letter is with Allah, which He has reserved for Himself in the knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب) with Him; and there is no power or strength except by Allah, the High, the Great."

In the same source, also with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made His Greatest Name consist of seventy-three letters. He gave Adam twenty-five letters of it, He gave Noah twenty-five letters of it, He gave Abraham eight letters of it, He gave Moses four letters of it, He gave Jesus two letters of it—by which he would bring the dead to life and heal the blind and the leper—and He gave Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seventy-two letters, and He veiled one letter so that what is in His Self would not be known—while He knows what is in the selves of others."

**I say:** Several other narrations follow the same course as these two. It should not be doubted that its being divided into seventy-three letters or composed of letters does not necessitate that it is literally composed of the letters of the alphabet, as previously alluded to. These two narrations contain an indication of that, for he counts the Name as one, then divides its letters among the Prophets and excludes one. If it were of the nature of verbal names whose total letters signify a single meaning, what was given to any of them (عليه‌السلام) would have been of no benefit to them at all.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission to ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) in a sermon of his: "Indeed, my Lord is Subtle in subtlety (لطيف اللطافة), yet He is not described by 'subtlety' [as a quality]? Great in greatness (عظيم العظمة), yet He is not described by 'greatness'; Grand in grandeur (كبير الكبرياء), yet He is not described by 'grandeur'; Majestic in majesty (جليل الجلالة), yet He is not described by 'thickness' (الغلظ). He is before everything, yet it is not said that anything was before Him; and He is after everything, yet it is not said of Him 'after.' He willed things without a thought (همة), He is Perceptive (دراك) without deception. He is in all things without being intermingled with them, and not distinct from them. He is Manifest (ظاهر) without the interpretation of physical contact, Self-disclosing (متجل) without the beginning of a vision, Distinct (بائن) without distance, Near (قريب) without proximity, Subtle (لطيف) without embodiment, Existent (موجود) not after non-existence, an Agent (جاعل) not by necessity, a Determiner (مقدر) without movement, a Willer (مريد) without concern, All-Hearing (سميع) without an instrument, All-Seeing (بصير) without a tool."

**I say:** He (عليه‌السلام)—as is observed—affirms the essential meanings (أصل المعاني) in His attributes and names, while negating the specificities of contingent referents and the deficiencies of matter. This is what we previously explained. These meanings are present in very many traditions narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), especially what has been reported from ‘Ali, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, al-Kazim, and al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) in many sermons. Whoever desires them should refer to the compendiums of Hadith, and Allah is the Guide.

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain of transmission to Hannan ibn Sadir from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition: "He has no likeness, no peer, and no equal. To Allah belong the Beautiful Names by which no other is named. They are those which Allah described in the Book, saying: **فَادْعُوهُ بِها وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمائِهِ** (so invoke Him by them, and leave those who blaspheme His names. [7:180]) out of ignorance without knowledge—and he does not know and disbelieves while thinking he is doing good? That is His saying: **وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** (And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him. [12:106]). They are those who blaspheme His names without knowledge, so they place them where they do not belong."

**I say:** The tradition supports what we previously mentioned regarding the meaning of the names being "beautiful" and "blaspheming" (الإلحاد) in them. His saying (عليه‌السلام): "no other is named by them" means that no one other than Him, the Exalted, is described by the meanings for which they were stripped [of deficiencies] and by which it became correct to name Him, such as applying "the Creator" (الخالق) in its true meaning—which belongs to Him, the Exalted—to another, and so on.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى فَادْعُوهُ بِها** (To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them. [7:180]), he said: "By Allah, we are the Beautiful Names—those from whom Allah does not accept [deeds] from the servants except through our recognition (معرفتنا)."

**I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from him (عليه‌السلام). In it, the "name" is taken in the sense of what points to a thing, whether it be a word or otherwise. Accordingly, the Prophets and the Successors (عليه‌السلام) are names pointing to Him, the Exalted, and intermediaries between Him and His creation. Because they are in a state of servitude (العبودية) such that they have nothing but Allah, the Glorified, they are the manifestors of His names and attributes, the Exalted.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** (Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and establish justice thereby. [7:181]). He said: 'They are the Imams.'"

**I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from Humran from him (عليه‌السلام). He said: And Muhammad ibn ‘Ajlan narrated from him (عليه‌السلام): "We are they." What supports this has already preceded in the previous commentary.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from al-Rabi‘ regarding His saying: **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ**, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, among my Ummah are a people upon the truth—until Jesus son of Mary descends, whenever he descends.'"

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad, from al-Sirri, from Ibn al-Mundhir, from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id, from his father, from Aban ibn Taghlib, from Fadl, from ‘Abd al-Malik al-Hamdani, from Zadan, from ‘Ali, who said: "This Ummah will split into seventy-three sects—seventy-two in the Fire, and one in Paradise, and they are those about whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ**—myself and my followers (شيعتي)."

**I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated from Zadan from him (عليه‌السلام) the like of it, and at its end: "and they are upon the truth" in place of his saying: "myself and my followers." This has already preceded under the saying of the Exalted: **وَمِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** (Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide by the truth and establish justice thereby. [7:159]), in the narration of al-Ayyashi from Abu al-Sahba’ from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) with its meaning, as well as the narration of al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through various paths from him.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to Sufyan ibn al-Simt, who said: "Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said: 'When Allah intends good for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with an affliction and reminds him to seek forgiveness. And when He intends evil for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with a blessing to make him forget seeking forgiveness and he persists in it; and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** (We will draw them on [to ruin] degree by degree from whence they know not. [7:182])—by blessings during acts of disobedience.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Sama‘ah ibn Mihran, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah: **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ**. He said: 'It is the servant who commits a sin, so a blessing is renewed for him along with it—that blessing distracts him from seeking forgiveness for that sin.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated with its chain of transmission from Ibn Ri’ab from some of our companions from him (عليه‌السلام) the like of it.

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to al-Hasan al-Sayqal, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about what the people narrate: 'Contemplation for an hour is better than standing [in prayer] for a night.' I said: 'How should one contemplate?' He said: 'He passes by a ruin or a house and says: Where are your inhabitants? Where are your builders? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?'"

**I say:** This is of the nature of showing some of the manifest referents (المصاديق).

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Mu‘ammar ibn Khallad, who said: "I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) saying: 'Worship (العبادة) is not the abundance of prayer and fasting. Rather, worship is contemplation (التفكر) on the affair of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to al-Rab‘i, who said: "Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said: 'The Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) said: Contemplation (التفكر) invites toward righteousness (البر) and acting upon it.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Muhammad ibn Abi al-Nasr from some of his men from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The best worship is the addiction to contemplation (إدمان التفكر) on Allah and His power."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding the interpretation of the saying of the Exalted: **وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ** (and He leaves them to wander blindly in their rebellion. [7:186]), he said: "He said: He entrusts him to himself (نكله إلى نفسه)."

**I say:** The meaning of leaving them to wander blindly in their rebellion is the absence of aiding them against themselves and leaving them to their own devices by cutting off divine success (التوفيق); thus, it aligns with being entrusted to one's self.
